savedState.boardInfo: Proof of Concept - Not Endorsed. V1.4.1b alignment rebuild researched from public DOC material available on 20 July 2026. The How-link graph was rebuilt by explicit source-side and target-side candidate review, without target-coverage fallback or nominal-owner assignment. It maps causal hypotheses, workstreams, teams/functions and cross-cutting competencies for discussion and review. It is not official DOC policy, a statutory plan, an accountability matrix, a performance finding or a substitute for engagement with Treaty partners. See “Board basis, sources and cautions” and “Alignment review and mapping cautions”.

pageInfo.overview: Agency-wide proof-of-concept outcomes map based on public DOC information available on 20 July 2026. Pages separate materially different conservation, partnership, visitor, regulatory and organisational pathways.
pageInfo.p1: A diagnostic-heavy investment pathway grounded in DOC’s Biodiversity Investment Approach and emerging integrated performance framework.
pageInfo.p2: Species recovery requires sustained treatment and enough time for population response; links express a draft causal model rather than proof of attribution.
pageInfo.p3: A branching mountains-to-sea pathway covering terrestrial, freshwater, marine and island systems without assuming one intervention fits every ecosystem.
pageInfo.p4: Protected-area and heritage stewardship balances statutory duties, cultural values, asset lifecycles, climate exposure, public access and good-neighbour expectations.
pageInfo.p5: Treaty partnership is modelled as active protection, early participation, authorised knowledge use, shared delivery and accountable relationships.
pageInfo.p6: The pathway follows the Heritage and Visitor Strategy hierarchy: protect heritage first, enable connection, then support people and communities to thrive.
pageInfo.p7: Collective conservation depends on iwi/hapū, communities, volunteers, businesses, landowners and public agencies; contribution is not treated as DOC delivery alone.
pageInfo.p8: Regulatory modernisation is modelled with both timeliness and conservation safeguards so speed is not mistaken for quality.
pageInfo.p9: Internal capability is placed last and linked back to field delivery, evidence, safety, assets, data, finance and partner experience.
pageInfo.p10: How level 1 lists agency-wide workstreams and services. Every box is mapped to all direct This–Then outcomes accepted in the candidate audit.
pageInfo.p11: How level 2 uses current published DOC teams/functions and aggregated regional-operations groupings. Every box is mapped upward to all directly relevant level 1 workstreams accepted in the candidate audit.
pageInfo.p12: This no-level Cross-Link page contains transferable competencies. Every competency has direct Cross-Links to relevant How boxes and This–Then boxes.
pageInfo.p13: Documents scope, public source basis, assumptions, cautions and review needs.
pageInfo.p14: Illustrative monitoring and evaluation plan using live Documentation Page clones of outcomes, Measures and Evaluation Questions.
pageInfo.p15: Records the candidate-by-candidate alignment method, structural signals, cautions and expert-review needs for the no-priorities mapping copy.

docContent.p13:
Purpose and scope

This is an AI-assisted proof-of-concept DoView Board of the New Zealand Department of Conservation Te Papa Atawhai. It maps plausible causal contributions across DOC’s current published outcomes and major functions; it is not an official DOC strategy, operating model, appropriation structure, performance judgement or endorsement.

Public source basis

The structure was researched on 20 July 2026 using current public DOC material. The 2025–2029 Strategic Intentions supplies the five final outcomes, current priorities and major programmes. The 2024/25 Annual Report informs the transition toward impact measures. Action for Nature, the Outcome Monitoring Framework, Heritage and Visitor Strategy, Conservation Act role, current business-group page and Predator Free 2050 material inform the domain pathways and implementation map.

  • DOC purpose and outcomes
  • DOC Strategic Intentions 2025–2029
  • DOC Annual Report for the year ended 30 June 2025
  • Action for Nature biodiversity implementation plan
  • National Outcome Monitoring Framework for biodiversity
  • DOC Heritage and Visitor Strategy
  • Conservation Act 1987 — DOC role
  • DOC current business groups
  • Predator Free 2050 strategic documents

Key assumptions

  • Box-and-link causality is a planning hypothesis to test, not proof that DOC alone causes the outcome.
  • National programmes, regions, Treaty partners, communities, businesses, landowners, other agencies and environmental conditions interact with these pathways.
  • Level 2 regional-operations boxes aggregate the published regional structure to keep the How page usable; detailed regional ownership should be confirmed by DOC.
  • Competencies are an analytical cross-cut, not a published DOC competency framework, except where they reflect capabilities explicitly discussed in public material.
  • Measures are illustrative and need definitions, baselines, disaggregation, frequency, ownership, targets and burden checks before use.

Cautions and review needs

  • Government priorities, leadership, business groups, programmes, funding and regulatory settings can change after the research date.
  • Te Tiriti o Waitangi, settlement commitments, mātauranga Māori and taonga relationships require direct review with the relevant Treaty partners; this public-information draft cannot substitute for engagement or permission.
  • Climate, ecological and population responses involve lags, uncertainty, thresholds and external drivers that are simplified in a visual board.
  • Human review is required before policy, funding, statutory, operational, public communication or evaluation use.
  • Opening this standalone HTML runs JavaScript locally. Board Chat is optional; for public-sector, sensitive or regulated use, keep it disabled unless an approved endpoint and data-handling controls exist.
docContent.p14:
Purpose

This illustrative plan links monitoring and evaluation to the board’s causal claims. Owners, definitions, baselines, targets, frequency, data quality, equity/Treaty review, cost and reporting routes must be agreed before use.

Outcome frame

Priority decision pathway

Illustrative Measures

Illustrative Evaluation Questions

Learning cycle

  1. Review the Measures with Treaty partners, delivery teams and affected communities.
  2. Interpret trends alongside implementation quality, context and unintended effects.
  3. Use Evaluation Questions to test priority causal links and alternative explanations.
  4. Record decisions to continue, adapt, stop or scale work, and update this board.
docContent.p15:
Mapping method

The V1.4.1b rebuild removed the former uncovered-target fallback. Each of the 18 Level 1 workstreams was compared with all 168 This–Then boxes, then each This–Then target was reverse-checked against all 18 workstreams. Each of the 18 Level 2 teams/functions was compared with all 18 Level 1 workstreams and reverse-checked. Each of the 15 no-level competencies was compared with every potentially relevant This–Then, Level 1 and Level 2 target and then target-side checked. A link was retained only where the relationship was judged direct, material and defensible as delivery, enablement, support, supply, governance or assurance.

The resulting graph has 1969 How links: 674 Level 1 → This–Then Vertical Links, 245 Level 2 → Level 1 Vertical Links and 1050 no-level Cross-Links. Incoming Level 1 distribution across This–Then boxes: zero 0, one 3, two 32, three 44, four or more 89.

Level 1 workstreams with the least Level 2 support

No minimum-support quota was used. The following are the five lowest structural counts and require organisational review before being interpreted as insufficient support.

  • Modernise digital and data services — 4 contributing Level 2 teams/functions
  • Run biodiversity investment portfolio — 11 contributing Level 2 teams/functions
  • Grow conservation partnerships and revenue — 12 contributing Level 2 teams/functions
  • Strengthen compliance and law enforcement — 12 contributing Level 2 teams/functions
  • Mobilise communities and volunteers — 13 contributing Level 2 teams/functions

Major concentrations of Level 1 support

  • Health and safety embedded — 10 incoming Level 1 links
  • Restoration gains enduring — 9 incoming Level 1 links
  • Conservation impact maximised — 8 incoming Level 1 links
  • Lands and waters maintained — 8 incoming Level 1 links
  • National and place plans aligned — 8 incoming Level 1 links
  • Outcomes monitored consistently — 8 incoming Level 1 links
  • Partners experience joined-up service — 8 incoming Level 1 links
  • Place condition improved — 8 incoming Level 1 links

Evidence gaps and cautions

  • Public sources describe strategy and high-level functions, not a complete responsibility matrix, staffing model, expenditure ledger or region-by-region delivery arrangement.
  • The three regional Level 2 boxes are analytical aggregations of the published regional structure.
  • Broad enabling functions and competencies can legitimately have many links; counts alone cannot distinguish shallow availability from adequate capability.
  • Some links remain uncertain where public material does not show the operating hand-off, decision right or intensity of contribution.
  • Link counts indicate possible alignment patterns but do not prove staffing, expenditure, accountability, ownership or causal strength.
  • Do not add or remove a link merely to match a priority or preferred distribution.

Required review: This mapping must be reviewed with DOC subject-matter experts, mana whenua and organisational leaders before operational, funding, accountability or assurance use.
docContent.p16:
Using DoView Boards and Disclaimer

See the disclaimer section below.

The DoView Board prompt and the DoView Board built within this DoView Board prototype app are provided free of charge so that anyone can experiment with DoView Boards and explore for themselves how they can answer the 20 key questions that anyone running or overseeing an organization needs to answer. See https://doviewplanning.org/doviewboardsuse.

Walk-Through

To get a walk-through for using DoView Boards, see https://doviewplanning.org/walkthrough.

Accessing Consulting or Training

We can provide consulting or training on integrating the use of DoView Boards into your existing planning, implementation and reporting workflows in business, government, or nonprofit settings. See https://doviewplanning.org/offerings.

Adapting the DoView Board Prototype App to your setting

This DoView Board is being used within the DoView Board prototype app. It is a free of charge app that runs inside any browser window and can be used for piloting, testing, proof-of-concept and for use where confidential information is not being included and where there is a low security risk.

The DoView Board app is an open-source developer project under an Apache-2.0 open-source license. See https://github.com/doviewplanning/official-doview-boards. The wider DoView Planning methodology and DoView® trademarks are governed separately. See https://doviewplanning.org/trademarkuse.

Because it is an open-source project, any developer can extend, harden, adapt, or include features of the DoView Board app in other planning products or systems to suit specific deployment requirements. For example, adding enhanced stability, scalability, security, authentication, data handling, or integration features needed for production use, sensitive content, or higher-security environments. If you are looking to deploy DoView Boards in a live operational setting, you can work with any developer to tailor the app to your needs. You can also get in touch with us for advice and methodological input into the best way for you to adapt and extend your use of DoView Boards in your particular setting. https://doviewplanning.org/contact.

Disclaimer

DoView Boards are provided for planning and illustrative purposes only. The content of any DoView Board does not constitute professional advice of any kind, including but not limited to legal, financial, medical, strategic, or organizational advice. No warranty is given as to the accuracy, completeness, or fitness for purpose of any content. The usual precautions for AI-generated material need to be taken. Dr Paul Duignan, DoViewPlanning.org, The Ideas Web Ltd, DoView Corporation Ltd and any associated parties accept no liability whatsoever for any loss, damage, or adverse outcome arising directly or indirectly from the use of, or reliance on, any DoView Board or its contents. Use entirely at your own risk.

Please note that this DoView Board is in an interactive HTML file and contains active JavaScript so the board can work. Only open boards from sources you trust. Read-only copies disable editing through the board interface but are not security protection.

measures.title: Threatened species population trend at managed sites
measures.mainText: Track survival, recruitment, abundance or occupancy using species-appropriate methods and intervals.

measures.title: Representative ecosystem condition and trend
measures.mainText: Use compatible terrestrial, freshwater and marine indicators at appropriate spatial scales.

measures.title: Priority area with threats effectively controlled
measures.mainText: Report treatment coverage alongside ecological response and sustained-control quality.

measures.title: Public conservation assets meeting service standards
measures.mainText: Combine condition, safety, accessibility and lifecycle information rather than counting assets alone.

measures.title: Priority cultural heritage places actively managed
measures.mainText: Track risk-based active management and condition of agreed priority places.

measures.title: Treaty partners reporting effective participation
measures.mainText: Use jointly agreed qualitative and quantitative feedback; avoid substituting meeting counts for partnership quality.

measures.title: Visitor benefit balanced with place impacts
measures.mainText: Pair visitor experience and connection evidence with ecological, cultural and crowding impacts.

measures.title: Third-party conservation investment mobilised
measures.mainText: Record additional cash and in-kind value with attribution and displacement cautions.

measures.title: Concession processing timeliness and decision quality
measures.mainText: Monitor end-to-end time, rework, statutory quality, conditions and applicant certainty.

measures.title: Staff engagement and safety performance
measures.mainText: Combine engagement, critical-risk, incident-learning and workforce-capability information.

measures.title: Monitoring coverage against shared indicators
measures.mainText: Track priority outcomes with current, comparable and decision-relevant evidence.

measures.title: Climate adaptation actions at high-risk places
measures.mainText: Track assessed high-risk places with funded, implemented and reviewed adaptation pathways.

evalQuestions.questionText: Which investment choices produce the largest enduring conservation gains?
evalQuestions.mainText: Compare additional impact, durability, distribution, feasibility and opportunity cost.

evalQuestions.questionText: Which threat controls are actually leading to species recovery?
evalQuestions.mainText: Test treatment coverage, biological response, lag time, interactions and counterfactual explanations.

evalQuestions.questionText: Which ecosystems remain least resilient, and why?
evalQuestions.mainText: Examine pressure combinations, climate exposure, connectivity and restoration constraints.

evalQuestions.questionText: Are land and heritage trade-offs protecting the values that matter?
evalQuestions.mainText: Review statutory, cultural, ecological, visitor, risk and lifecycle consequences.

evalQuestions.questionText: How consistently do active protection and Treaty participation occur?
evalQuestions.mainText: Look across places and decisions, including partner-defined quality and early influence.

evalQuestions.questionText: Who benefits from visitor opportunities, and who misses out?
evalQuestions.mainText: Assess access, experience, community benefit, displacement and place impacts.

evalQuestions.questionText: Does collective action add durable capacity or shift hidden costs?
evalQuestions.mainText: Test additionality, volunteer burden, coordination costs, local ownership and continuity.

evalQuestions.questionText: Does regulatory modernisation improve timeliness without weakening safeguards?
evalQuestions.mainText: Examine processing time, decision quality, effects avoided, compliance and Treaty practice together.

evalQuestions.questionText: What enables field staff to spend more time for nature?
evalQuestions.mainText: Investigate digital friction, approvals, asset support, workload, safety and decision rights.

evalQuestions.questionText: How should evidence and partner knowledge change the next approach?
evalQuestions.mainText: Use monitoring, evaluation, mātauranga and operational learning to revise portfolios and methods.
